name: bdi-mental-states
description: This skill should be used when the user asks to "model agent mental states", "implement BDI architecture", "create belief-desire-intention models", "transform RDF to beliefs", "build cognitive agent", or mentions BDI ontology, mental state modeling, rational agency, or neuro-symbolic AI integration.
capabilities: Provides BDI ontology patterns for belief-desire-intention modeling
Includes RDF/Turtle examples for mental state representation
Offers SPARQL queries for competency validation
Documents integration patterns for SEMAS and JADE frameworks
Contains temporal reasoning patterns for mental state evolution
author: muratcankoylan
securitySummary: Documentation-only skill containing theoretical BDI ontology patterns. Static findings are false positives triggered by markdown code fences (mistaken as shell commands) and semantic web IRIs (mistaken as network operations). No executable code, system operations, or actual cryptographic implementations exist. The skill is purely instructional content for cognitive architecture modeling.
valueStatement: Transform RDF data into cognitive agent architectures using formal Belief-Desire-Intention patterns. Enable AI systems to model rational agency with traceable reasoning chains and semantic interoperability for multi-agent systems.
userTitle: Model AI Agent Mental States with BDI Ontology
limitations: Documentation only - no executable implementation provided
Requires external RDF/SPARQL tools for actual processing
Ontology examples need adaptation for specific domains

useCases.title: Implement Cognitive Architectures
useCases.description: Design BDI-based reasoning systems for autonomous agents with traceable mental states

useCases.title: Model Agent Beliefs in RDF
useCases.description: Create RDF graphs representing agent mental states for knowledge-based systems

useCases.title: Enable Agent Communication
useCases.description: Implement belief sharing and coordination protocols using formal ontologies

promptTemplates.title: Basic BDI Modeling
promptTemplates.scenario: Transform simple scenarios into BDI mental states
promptTemplates.prompt: Given this scenario: [describe situation], create RDF triples representing the agent beliefs, desires, and intentions using BDI ontology patterns

promptTemplates.title: Multi-Agent Coordination
promptTemplates.scenario: Model shared beliefs across multiple agents
promptTemplates.prompt: Design BDI mental states for agents that need to coordinate on [task]. Include shared beliefs and complementary intentions

promptTemplates.title: Temporal Reasoning
promptTemplates.scenario: Track mental state evolution over time
promptTemplates.prompt: Create BDI mental states that change over time for this sequence: [timeline]. Include validity intervals and process triggers

promptTemplates.title: Conflict Resolution
promptTemplates.prompt: Given conflicting beliefs [list beliefs] and intentions [list intentions], model the resolution process using BDI justification patterns

bestPractices: Always link mental states to world states via refersTo relationships for grounding
Include temporal validity intervals for all mental entities to support diachronic reasoning
Use bidirectional properties (motivates/isMotivatedBy) for flexible querying
Provide explicit justifications for each mental state to enable explainability
antiPatterns: Conflating mental states with world states - beliefs reference but are not world states
Missing temporal bounds - every mental state needs validity intervals
Flat belief structures - use compositional modeling with hasPart for complex beliefs
Direct intention-to-action mapping - intentions specify plans which contain tasks

faq.question: Is this skill compatible with existing BDI frameworks?
faq.answer: Yes, it provides integration patterns for SEMAS, JADE/JADEX, and other BDI platforms through ontology mapping

faq.question: What are the limitations of the BDI approach?
faq.answer: BDI models require explicit representation of all mental states and may not capture subconscious or emotional factors

faq.question: How do I integrate this with my existing RDF data?
faq.answer: Use the T2B2T pattern: translate your RDF to beliefs, apply BDI reasoning, then project back to RDF

faq.question: Is my data safe when using this ontology?
faq.answer: This is documentation only - no data processing occurs. Implementations should validate RDF inputs and use secure SPARQL endpoints

faq.question: How do I handle inconsistent mental states?
faq.answer: Use justification tracking to identify conflicts, then apply resolution strategies like temporal partitioning or belief revision

faq.question: How does this compare to other cognitive architectures?
faq.answer: BDI focuses on practical reasoning with clear mental state distinctions, making it more interpretable than neural approaches
